keeperNote: 2026-06-04 (The Keeper): completed the shared NSAID drug-set across all NSAID-combo rules — added dexibuprofen, tenoxicam, sulindac, dexketoprofen, tiaprofenic acid, tolfenamic acid and fenoprofen, which were previously absent and so silently never fired any NSAID PINCER alert. The same completion was applied to the built-in NSAIDS list in content-scripts/triage-lens/content.js (the list that actually fires the shipping prescribing-flag chips). NSAID lists corroborated via search; pending primary-source BNF confirmation. 2026-06-06 (The Keeper): updated valproate notes (MHRA June 2025 updated PPP materials) and isotretinoin notes (MHRA Jan 2026 GP prescribing regulatory changes — GPs may now prescribe to <18s without second prescriber agreement). Two new MHRA DSU alerts proposed but not yet applied pending primary-source confirmation: GLP-1 pancreatitis alert (MHRA DSU 29 Jan 2026) and semaglutide NAION alert (MHRA DSU 5 Feb 2026) — see The Keeper change-proposal report for details.
notes: Starter library of prescribing-safety and clinical alerts for UK primary care. Based on PINCER (BMJ 2012, doi:10.1136/bmj.e6501) and UK prescribing safety guidelines. Each rule is an editable starting point — GPs should review match terms and thresholds against their local formulary and patient population. See individual notes fields for clinical justification and known limitations.

library.title: NSAID without gastroprotection (≥65)
library.description: PINCER #1: patient aged ≥65 prescribed a systemic NSAID without a co-prescribed PPI or H2-blocker. The absence-of-gastroprotection half cannot be fully expressed with a single drug-combo set; this rule fires on NSAID presence in ≥65s and flags review. Enable the mustNotBePresent variant below if your formulary is consistent.

rule.label: NSAID without gastroprotection (≥65)
rule.notes: PINCER #1. Co-prescribe PPI (first line) or H2-blocker if NSAID must be continued. Consider whether NSAID can be stopped. mustNotBePresent list covers PPIs and H2-blockers — rule fires only when NONE of these are co-prescribed.

library.title: Anticoagulant + NSAID concurrent
library.description: PINCER #2: patient prescribed an oral anticoagulant (warfarin or DOAC) AND a systemic NSAID — major GI bleed risk.

rule.label: Anticoagulant + NSAID concurrent
rule.notes: PINCER #2. Concurrent anticoagulant + systemic NSAID markedly increases GI bleed risk. Consider stopping NSAID; ensure PPI co-prescribed if continuation unavoidable.

library.title: Aspirin + NSAID concurrent (no PPI)
library.description: PINCER #3: concurrent aspirin and systemic NSAID without gastroprotection — additive GI bleed risk.

rule.label: Aspirin + NSAID concurrent (no PPI)
rule.notes: PINCER #3. Aspirin + NSAID combination carries significantly elevated GI bleed risk. Co-prescription of PPI is strongly recommended if both drugs must be continued. Review indication for NSAID.

library.title: ACE inhibitor or ARB + NSAID concurrent
library.description: PINCER #4: concurrent RAAS blocker (ACEi/ARB) + systemic NSAID — risk of acute kidney injury.

rule.label: ACEi/ARB + NSAID concurrent
rule.notes: PINCER #4. 'Triple whammy' risk if diuretic also present. NSAIDs reduce renal perfusion synergistically with RAAS blockade. Counsel sick-day rules; review NSAID indication.

library.title: Beta-blocker + verapamil or diltiazem concurrent
library.description: PINCER #5: concurrent beta-blocker and rate-limiting calcium channel blocker — risk of bradycardia and heart block.

rule.label: Beta-blocker + rate-limiting CCB
rule.notes: PINCER #5. Combination causes additive negative chronotropic and dromotropic effects. Risk of severe bradycardia, AV block, and asystole. Usually contraindicated unless under specialist supervision (e.g. refractory angina).

library.title: NSAID in heart failure
library.description: PINCER #6: systemic NSAID prescribed in a patient with a heart failure diagnosis — fluid retention worsens HF.

rule.label: NSAID in heart failure
rule.notes: PINCER #6. NSAIDs cause sodium and water retention, worsen cardiac function, and antagonise the effect of loop diuretics. Avoid in heart failure; paracetamol is preferred for analgesia.

library.title: Warfarin without recent INR (90 days)
library.description: PINCER #7: patient on warfarin with no INR recorded in the last 90 days — INR monitoring overdue.

rule.label: Warfarin without recent INR
rule.notes: PINCER #7. INR must be within therapeutic range for warfarin to be safe. Monitoring frequency varies by stability — 90 days is the maximum acceptable gap for stable patients.

library.title: Aspirin + clopidogrel without PPI (dual antiplatelet)
library.description: PINCER #8: dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel/ticagrelor/prasugrel) without PPI gastroprotection.

rule.label: Dual antiplatelet without PPI
rule.notes: PINCER #8. NICE recommends PPI co-prescription with dual antiplatelet therapy. Review after 12 months — dual antiplatelet is usually time-limited post-ACS or PCI.

library.title: Metformin without recent renal function check
library.description: PINCER #9: patient on metformin with no U&E/eGFR in the last 12 months — lactic acidosis risk if eGFR <30.

rule.label: Metformin — annual eGFR overdue
rule.notes: PINCER #9. Metformin is contraindicated when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² and should be used with caution <45. Annual renal function monitoring is essential. Stop/withhold during acute illness, dehydration, or before iodinated contrast.

library.title: Loop diuretic without recent U&E (6 months)
library.description: PINCER #10: patient on a loop diuretic (furosemide/bumetanide) without U&E check in 6 months — hypokalaemia and electrolyte disturbance risk.

rule.label: Loop diuretic — 6-monthly U&E overdue
rule.notes: PINCER #10. Loop diuretics cause electrolyte loss — especially hypokalaemia and hyponatraemia. 6-monthly U&E is the minimum; more frequent if frail, high dose, or renal impairment.

library.title: Amiodarone without 6-monthly TFT/LFT
library.description: PINCER #11: patient on amiodarone without thyroid and liver function tests in 6 months — amiodarone-induced thyroid and hepatic toxicity.

rule.label: Amiodarone — 6-monthly TFT/LFT overdue
rule.notes: PINCER #11. Amiodarone causes both hypo- and hyperthyroidism and hepatotoxicity. 6-monthly TFT and LFT are mandatory. CXR annually for pulmonary toxicity.

library.title: Lithium + NSAID concurrent
library.description: PINCER #12: concurrent lithium and systemic NSAID — NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance causing toxicity.

rule.label: Lithium + NSAID concurrent
rule.notes: PINCER #12. NSAIDs reduce renal lithium excretion by up to 25%, raising levels into the toxic range (>1.5 mmol/L). Recommend paracetamol as first-line analgesia for patients on lithium. If NSAID unavoidable, check lithium level urgently and reduce dose.

library.title: Anticoagulant + antiplatelet (triple therapy) in ≥75s
library.description: PINCER #13: triple therapy (anticoagulant + dual antiplatelet) in patients aged ≥75 — very high bleeding risk.

rule.label: Triple antithrombotic therapy (≥75)
rule.notes: PINCER #13. Triple antithrombotic therapy in ≥75s carries very high bleeding risk. NICE recommends limiting dual antiplatelet + anticoagulant therapy duration (typically 1–4 weeks post-ACS/PCI) then stepping down to single agent. Review indication and duration urgently.

library.title: Valproate in female of childbearing age
library.description: Valproate (sodium valproate / valproic acid) in female aged 12–55 — MHRA Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) mandatory.

rule.label: Valproate — female childbearing age
rule.notes: MHRA Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP): valproate must not be prescribed to women/girls of childbearing potential without annual specialist review, confirmed effective contraception, and a signed PPP form (MHRA 2024 update). June 2025: MHRA issued updated PPP safety materials and educational resources; hard copies distributed from July 2025. Rule logic unchanged. Check PPP compliance annually. Valproate causes major congenital malformations (10%) and neurodevelopmental disorders (30–40%) in children exposed in utero.

library.title: Lithium without 3-monthly level + 6-monthly U&E/TFT
library.description: Lithium monitoring overdue — serum level every 3 months, U&E and TFT every 6 months.

rule.label: Lithium — monitoring overdue
rule.notes: NICE CG185. Level should be taken 12 hours post-dose. Target 0.6–1.0 mmol/L (or 0.4–0.8 mmol/L in elderly). Symptoms of toxicity: coarse tremor, polyuria, vomiting, confusion.

library.title: SGLT2 inhibitor — DKA awareness reminder
library.description: Patient on SGLT2 inhibitor: reminder to check sick-day rules and DKA risk awareness (including euglycaemic DKA).

rule.label: SGLT2 inhibitor — DKA risk reminder
rule.notes: MHRA Drug Safety Updates 2020–2024. SGLT2 inhibitors carry risk of euglycaemic DKA (blood glucose may be near-normal). Sick-day rules: withhold during acute illness, dehydration, surgery, or prolonged fasting. Counsel on symptoms of DKA. Annual eGFR monitoring via drug-monitoring rule (sglt2-inhibitor). This alert is an informational prompt — review sick-day rule education at each medication review.

library.title: Isotretinoin in female of childbearing age
library.description: Isotretinoin (Roaccutane) in female aged 12–55 — Pregnancy Prevention Programme mandatory, initiated and managed by dermatology but GP must be aware.

rule.label: Isotretinoin — female childbearing age
rule.notes: Isotretinoin is highly teratogenic (Category X). The MHRA Pregnancy Prevention Programme requires two forms of contraception and monthly pregnancy tests. January 2026: GPs may now prescribe isotretinoin to patients under 18 without a second prescriber's agreement (regulatory update, MHRA Jan 2026); remote follow-up consultations and remotely-supervised pregnancy testing are now permitted. Alert logic unchanged: rule fires for all female patients aged 12-55 on isotretinoin. GP role: be aware the patient is on isotretinoin and that PPP compliance is being maintained.

library.title: Two or more QTc-prolonging drugs concurrent
library.description: Concurrent use of two or more drugs known to prolong the QT interval — additive risk of torsades de pointes.

rule.label: QTc-prolonging drug combination
rule.notes: Note: this rule will fire when any two drugs from the QTc list are co-prescribed, including the same drug in different formulations. Review the matchSummary in the chip to confirm these are genuinely two different QTc-prolonging agents. Consider ECG monitoring, especially if patient has hypokalaemia, bradycardia, or prior QTc prolongation.

library.title: Recurrent UTI (≥3 in 12 months, female <65)
library.description: Female patient aged under 65 with 3 or more UTI / cystitis problems coded in the last 12 months — consider recurrent UTI workup.

rule.label: Recurrent UTI (≥3 in 12 months)
rule.notes: NICE NG112: consider recurrent UTI if ≥2 in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months. Workup: MSU culture, urine dipstick, consider renal USS and referral. Low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis or vaginal oestrogen may be appropriate post-menopausally.

library.title: Recurrent falls (≥2 in 12 months, ≥65)
library.description: Patient aged ≥65 with 2 or more falls coded in the last 12 months — consider multifactorial falls assessment.

rule.label: Recurrent falls (≥2 in 12 months)
rule.notes: NICE CG161: offer multifactorial falls assessment to older people with ≥2 falls or one fall with injury in 12 months. Review medications (particularly those causing hypotension, sedation), vision, balance, home hazards. Consider medication review, physiotherapy, and home assessment.

library.title: High-risk GI bleed combination (NSAID + anticoagulant, ≥65, HF)
library.description: Example composite: fires when BOTH pincer-2 (anticoagulant + NSAID) AND pincer-6 (NSAID in HF) are active — i.e. the patient has all three risk factors simultaneously. Shows how composite rules combine others.

rule.label: NSAID + anticoagulant + HF — extreme GI bleed risk
rule.notes: Composite rule example. Replace the placeholder ruleIds above with the actual saved IDs of the pincer-2 (anticoagulant+NSAID) and pincer-6 (NSAID in HF) rules in your custom rules list after importing them. When both fire simultaneously this composite surfaces a consolidated high-severity alert.

library.title: Rising PSA trend (≥3 readings, 24 months)
library.description: PSA values showing a rising trend across ≥3 readings in 24 months with a minimum delta of 1.0 ng/mL — consider urology referral.

rule.notes: Observation history is required for trend analysis. Currently, only the latest PSA value is available via the investigation dashboard API. This rule will show no_data until multi-point history is available. When history is available: a PSA rise >1 ng/mL/year or PSA velocity >0.75 ng/mL/year warrants urology referral (NICE NG12).
